title: Test Variables Affecting Visual Test Results
narrationScript: Two inspectors look at the same weld. One finds the crack, one walks past it. The difference is rarely eyesight. It is variables, and Level Two work means controlling them on purpose. Start with equipment. Light intensity is the first control. Procedures commonly call for about five hundred lux for general examination and about one thousand lux where you make the call on critical surfaces. Numbers like that mean nothing unless you measure them where they matter, at the test surface, with a calibrated light meter. Flashlights dim as batteries sag, lamps age, and distance is brutal. Double the distance from light to surface and the illumination falls to roughly one quarter. The type of light matters as much as the amount. Cool white sources render colors differently than warm ones, and color tells you things. Heat tint, corrosion products, oxide films. Direction may matter most of all. Light skimming the surface at a low angle throws shadows from the smallest relief. The same crack that disappears under a flood light from above can jump out under raking light at fifteen degrees. Now the material. A rough as cast surface scatters light evenly but buries fine indications in texture. A polished surface gives mirror like reflections, and the glare can blind you until you move the light or move your head. Mill scale, oil, and old coatings hide the surface you are being paid to examine, which is why cleaning requirements are written into procedures. The discontinuity has its own say. Size, obviously. But also orientation. A tight crack viewed from the wrong direction shows almost nothing. Visual testing only sees what breaks the surface, and a crack smeared over by grinding or peening may not show at all. When you do find something, dimension it properly. Length comes from a rule or tape laid along the indication path, including the fine tails you only see under magnification. Width calls for a measuring magnifier with a graduated reticle, reading to a tenth of a millimeter. Depth needs a real instrument. A dial pit depth gauge that bridges the surface, a depth micrometer, or an undercut gauge on welds. There is also an old trick. With light at a known angle, the shadow length converts to depth through simple trigonometry. Whatever you use, record the units, the instrument, and its calibration status. Coverage is a variable too. Know whether your procedure demands one hundred percent examination or a sampling plan, and know what happens when a sampled item fails, because most plans expand the sample after a rejection. Scan in a systematic pattern with deliberate overlap between passes, mark what you have completed, and slow down. Scanning speed is one of the strongest predictors of what gets missed. Then there is you. Visual testing runs through a human instrument, and humans drift. Vigilance falls measurably after long sustained search, which is why rotation and breaks belong in the plan, not in your pride. Expectation bias is real. If you expect the part to be clean, you will tend to see it clean. Fatigue, time pressure, awkward access, and poor posture all push detection down. And if the work is inside a dark vessel, remember your eyes need as much as thirty minutes to adapt fully to low light. Keep your near vision examination current, because it is the annual proof that the human instrument still resolves fine detail. Last, keep the vocabulary straight, because it keeps your reports honest. Detection is perceiving that an indication exists. Interpretation is deciding what that indication is. False, nonrelevant, or relevant. Evaluation is comparing a relevant indication with the acceptance criteria. Only an indication that fails evaluation is a defect. A surface mark from a chipping hammer is an indication. Calling it nonrelevant is interpretation. Measuring a real undercut and comparing it to the limit in the code is evaluation. Use the words that way every time, and your paperwork will stand up to anyone who reads it.

sections.heading: Equipment Variables: The Light You Bring
sections.body:
Light intensity is the first equipment variable, and it is measurable. Procedures commonly require about **500 lux (50 fc)** for general examination and **1000 lux (100 fc)** for critical or final direct examination — verified with a **calibrated light meter at the test surface**, not at the lamp and not from memory. Watch the things that quietly steal intensity: battery droop in flashlights, aging lamps, dirty lenses, and distance — illuminance falls with the square of distance, so doubling lamp-to-surface distance cuts it to roughly one quarter.

Light **type** matters too. Sources with different color temperature and color-rendering quality change how heat tint, corrosion products, and oxide films read; a surface judged under warm incandescent light can look different under cool LED light. **Direction** is the inspector's lever: diffuse, even light for general survey; **raking light** — skimming the surface at a low angle — to throw shadows from shallow relief; repositioning to defeat glare on specular surfaces.

Finally, auxiliary equipment is itself a variable: scratched mirrors, filmed-over magnifier lenses, and borescopes that have not had a recent resolution check all degrade what reaches your eye. Verify the tools as deliberately as you verify the light.

sections.caption: The same shallow discontinuity under overhead flood light and under low-angle raking light — the shadow does the detection, and the meter verifies intensity at the surface.

sections.heading: Material and Discontinuity Variables
sections.body:
The part pushes back. **Surface finish variation** is the dominant material variable: a rough as-cast or as-welded surface scatters light diffusely and buries fine linear indications in texture noise, while a machined or polished surface reflects **specularly**, creating glare that hides everything until you move the light or your eye. Mill scale, oxide, oil, and old coatings conceal the actual surface — which is why procedures specify cleaning before examination. Base-material **color and reflectivity** set the contrast available between an indication and its background; a dark, tight crack on dark oxidized steel offers far less contrast than the same crack on bright machined stock.

The **discontinuity itself** contributes its own variables:

- **Size** — length and width relative to viewing distance and acuity.
- **Orientation** — a tight crack viewed end-on or along its plane shows almost nothing; broadside with shadowing, it stands out.
- **Surface connection** — VT sees only what breaks the surface; subsurface discontinuities are invisible without another method.
- **Condition** — open and oxide-stained cracks show well; tight fatigue cracks barely; cracks smeared over by grinding or peening may not show at all.

When finish, contrast, or smearing fight you, change the variables you control: light angle, magnification, cleaning — or call for another NDT method.

sections.heading: Determining Indication Dimensions
sections.body:
An indication is not reportable data until it has numbers on it — length, width, and depth, each with the right tool:

- **Length**: lay a flexible steel rule or tape along the indication path and record end-to-end, including hairline tails visible only under magnification. For a curved crack, follow the path, not the chord, unless the procedure says otherwise.
- **Width**: use a **measuring magnifier** with a graduated reticle (typically 0.1 mm divisions) or an optical comparator with interchangeable reticles laid over the indication.
- **Depth**: use a **dial pit-depth gauge** bridging the surrounding surface with its point in the cavity, a depth micrometer, or a weld undercut gauge of the V-WAC type. A machinist's rule across the surface with a feeler underneath works for wide depressions. The **shadow method** is the field trick: with light at a known elevation angle, depth equals shadow length multiplied by the tangent of that angle.

Record the units, the instrument used, and its calibration status with every measurement — a dimension without traceability is just an estimate. Where the surface itself is wavy, reference the gauge to the procedure-defined datum, not to the nearest high spot.

sections.caption: Pinning numbers on an indication: rule for length, graduated reticle for width, and a dial pit-depth gauge bridging the surface for depth.

sections.heading: Sampling and Scanning Variables
sections.body:
Coverage decisions change results as surely as lighting does. Know whether the procedure requires **100 % examination** or a **sampling plan** — a percentage or a fixed number per lot — and know the expansion rule: most plans require examining additional items, or the whole lot, once a sampled item rejects. Skipping that expansion invalidates the lot acceptance, and auditors know it.

Scanning technique is the moment-to-moment version of the same variable:

- **Speed** — scan rate is one of the strongest predictors of missed indications; slow down where criticality is high.
- **Pattern and overlap** — use a systematic raster or grid with deliberate overlap between passes; random wandering leaves unexamined stripes.
- **Geometry** — keep the eye within 24 in. (600 mm) and the line of sight at not less than 30° to the surface, repositioning rather than straining at shallow angles.
- **Marking** — mark completed zones and suspect areas as you go, and re-examine suspect zones with a changed light angle before you call them.

Dwell time on suspect areas, rest pauses, and a consistent start-and-finish routine make coverage reproducible — which is exactly what a procedure audit checks.

sections.heading: Personnel (Human Factors) Variables
sections.body:
Every visual test runs through a human being, and human performance drifts. The controllable factors:

- **Visual acuity** — near-vision examination (such as Jaeger J-1 or J-2 at a specified distance) at least annually per the written practice, and color-perception screening on entry. Wear the corrective lenses you tested with.
- **Vigilance decrement** — detection performance during sustained search measurably declines over time on task; rotation, breaks, and task variety belong in the inspection plan.
- **Expectation bias** — you tend to see what you expect. A 'clean' production history primes you to miss the exception; deliberately searching for specific discontinuity types counters it.
- **Fatigue, time pressure, and comfort** — awkward access, poor posture, heat, and schedule pressure all push detection probability down. Stage the work so the inspector is stable and the surface is presented well.
- **Adaptation** — moving from daylight into a dark vessel costs you: full dark adaptation takes up to about 30 minutes. Plan low-light work accordingly.

None of this is soft material — human-factors variables move detection rates as much as lighting does, and procedures increasingly say so.

sections.heading: Detection, Interpretation, Evaluation
sections.body:
Keep three words straight and your reports stay defensible.

**Detection** is perceiving that an indication exists at all. Everything in this lesson — light, surface, discontinuity geometry, scanning, human state — feeds the probability that a given discontinuity gets detected.

**Interpretation** is deciding what the detected indication is: **false** (an artifact of the test conditions, like a glare line), **nonrelevant** (real, but caused by something other than a discontinuity of concern — a machining mark, a weld ripple shadow), or **relevant** (caused by a discontinuity that the acceptance criteria address).

**Evaluation** comes only after interpretation: the relevant indication is measured and compared with the acceptance criteria of the governing code or specification. An indication that fails evaluation is a **defect** — and only then. Accepted relevant indications still get documented when the procedure requires it, because today's accepted indication is tomorrow's monitoring baseline.

sections.title: The Chain That Keeps Reports Honest
sections.body: **Indication → interpretation → evaluation → disposition.** Never write 'defect' in a report for anything that has not failed evaluation against named acceptance criteria. The word carries contractual and legal weight — let the criteria, not the adrenaline, decide.

figures.title: Light Angle Changes What You See
Determining Indication Length, Width, and Depth

interactive.title: Illumination Check
interactive.intro: Use the light-meter reading taken at the test surface to verify the examination area meets the procedure minimum — commonly 500 lux for general visual testing and 1000 lux for critical direct examination. If the reading falls short, move the light closer, add auxiliary lighting, or change the lamp, then re-measure at the surface.

checkQuestions.question: When verifying illumination for a visual examination, where must the light meter read?
options: Exactly one meter from the surface
checkQuestions.explanation: The requirement is illuminance on the surface being examined, so the meter sensor goes at the test surface. Readings at the lamp or the eye do not represent what the surface receives.

checkQuestions.question: Moving a flashlight from directly overhead to a 10–30° raking angle mainly improves detection of:
options: Shallow surface relief such as tight cracks, pits, and undercut
checkQuestions.explanation: Low-angle light throws long shadows from small surface relief, converting shallow topography into visible contrast. It does nothing for subsurface conditions, which VT cannot see.

checkQuestions.question: A polished, machined surface most often hurts visual testing because it:
options: Creates specular glare that hides indications until the light or viewing position changes
Absorbs all of the incident light
checkQuestions.explanation: Smooth surfaces reflect specularly, like a mirror; the glare band can completely mask indications until the inspector repositions the light source or line of sight.

checkQuestions.question: The best tool to measure the depth of an isolated corrosion pit is:
options: A flexible steel rule laid across the pit
A dial pit-depth gauge bridging the surrounding surface
checkQuestions.explanation: A pit-depth gauge spans the surrounding surface and drops a point to the pit bottom, reading depth directly. Rules measure length, fillet gauges measure weld size, and Hi-Lo gauges measure fit-up mismatch.

checkQuestions.question: Classifying a detected indication as false, nonrelevant, or relevant is called:
checkQuestions.explanation: Interpretation assigns meaning to a detected indication. Evaluation is the later step of comparing a relevant indication with acceptance criteria.

references: CP-105-2024 VT Topical Outline, Level II Supplement §4.1–4.8
Codes specifying VT lighting and viewing geometry (generic, e.g., ASME Section V Article 9 practice)
Human-factors literature on vigilance and inspection performance (generic reference)
